failure_modes.why: RED stayed on only 3-4 settlements and built cities very late, never generating enough natural resources to win.
RED spent the entire game churning 2:1/3:1 maritime trades (especially ORE→BRICK) instead of building a real production engine.
RED barely expanded its road/settlement network, getting boxed in while BLUE spread out to many nodes.

review_summary: RED relied on endless maritime trading rather than expanding settlements and upgrading cities, so its production never scaled to a 10-VP pace while BLUE out-expanded it.

0.summary: Settlement 25 is a fully defensible opening placement with zero oracle regret and solid diversity.

149.summary: A defensible early road expansion that builds toward future settlements, though a marginally better direction existed.

66.summary: A sound early road extension after a sensible 4:1 trade, only marginally off the oracle's preferred road direction.

219.summary: A solid robber move against the leading opponent with zero regret per the oracle.

282.summary: A reasonable cheap road build that extends RED's network with no meaningful regret versus the oracle's alternative.

351.summary: Cheap, zero-regret road build that secures longest road and uses surplus resources before discard risk.

failed.claude: Player is far behind at 3-7 yet chose a road instead of BUILD_SETTLEMENT 10, which directly scores a VP and adds production.
failed.openai: Building a settlement at node 10 would have provided immediate VP, which is crucial when far behind.

failed.claude: Spending wood+brick on a road yields no VP when a settlement (immediate VP) was legal and affordable with the current hand.
failed.openai: Building a settlement at node 10 would have been a more efficient use of resources for VP gain.

509.summary: RED wasted tempo on a road while a legal settlement at node 10 was available to score an immediate VP and boost production.

failed.claude: Trading 4 surplus wood for 1 brick is a defensible road-building move since wood is the player's most abundant resource, though ore would have aided cities.
failed.openai: Traded for brick when ore was more valuable due to lack of ore and need for cities.

failed.claude: Resulting hand (2 brick, 4 wood... after trade 0 wood, 2 brick) supports a road build, advancing the longest-road/expansion plan.
failed.openai: Trading for ore would have advanced towards a city, a key build, unlike brick.

432.summary: A reasonable 4:1 bank trade of surplus wood for a usable brick that advances builds without aiding the opponent, though grabbing ore for cities may have been marginally better.

578.summary: A defensible 2:1 bank trade of ORE for BRICK to fuel expansion while behind, though it depletes RED's ore for any city/dev plan.

717.summary: A reasonable port trade dumping surplus brick, though grabbing WHEAT instead of SHEEP would better advance a needed build.

655.summary: A reasonable 4:1 dump of surplus brick for needed wheat that trims the hand before discard with no downside.

793.summary: A clean port trade of surplus ORE for needed WHEAT that constructively builds toward VP with no downside.

864.summary: The trade effectively converts excess ORE into a more versatile resource (WHEAT) without aiding the opponent.

930.summary: Converting surplus sheep into needed ore at the bank is a reasonable late-game move toward a city.

967.summary: Converting excess wood to needed wheat via the bank is the oracle-optimal, low-risk trade.
